project_overview.project_name: HOPE for Quality Basic Education for All (HOPE-EDU)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve foundational learning outcomes, increase access to basic education and enhance education systems in participating States
project_overview.total_financing: US$552.18 million (IDA Credit: US$500 million; GPE Grant: US$52.18 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: 29 million children in public primary schools; 500,000 public primary teachers; 65,000+ public primary schools; 1.5 million out-of-school children in non-formal education; government agencies at federal, state and local levels

primary_locations: 15 States with largest OOSC populations (RA2: Increasing Access)
All 36 States and FCT (RA3: Enhancing Key Systems)
geographic_scope.coverage_type: national with state-level participation based on eligibility criteria
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Northern Nigeria zones have 82-93% learning deprivation rates; security concerns cited by 1.6-3.1% of families; Boko Haram targeting of female students in northern Nigeria; operation marked as Fragile State(s) in datasheet

key_components.component_name: Results Area 1: Improving Quality (Structured Pedagogy Program)
key_components.description: Support foundational literacy and numeracy through structured pedagogy packages including teaching-learning materials (TLMs), teacher training, mentoring, and learning assessments. Includes textbook supply chain strengthening with track-and-trace systems.
key_components.financing: US$263 million (IDA: US$233.527 million; GPE: US$29.473 million)

key_components.description: Create 13,000 new primary classrooms through community-government partnerships and establish 10,000 non-formal learning centers (NFLCs) for out-of-school children. Includes climate-resilient infrastructure standards.
key_components.financing: US$127.5 million (IDA: US$120.067 million; GPE: US$7.433 million)

key_components.description: Strengthen decentralized management of UBE funds, implement annual school grants for primary schools, enhance school management and governance, and digitalize Annual School Census (ASC) system.
key_components.financing: US$129.25 million (IDA: US$120.070 million; GPE: US$9.180 million)

key_components.description: Support design and delivery of critical activities through TA, capacity building, equipment and materials. Includes support for structured pedagogy quality assurance, EMIS strengthening, partnership agreement design, and independent verification.
key_components.financing: US$32.43 million (IDA: US$26.336 million; GPE: US$6.094 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Education (FME), Universal Basic Education Commission (UBEC), State Universal Basic Education Boards (SUBEBs), State Ministries of Education (SMEs), Local Government Education Authorities (LGEAs)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Program Steering Committee (NPSC) chaired by Minister of Education; State Program Steering Committees (SPSCs) in each participating state; Inter-Ministerial HOPE Steering Committee for coordination across HOPE-EDU, HOPE-GOV, and HOPE-PHC operations; National Program Coordinator (NPC) at FME; Program Manager at UBEC; technical experts at federal and state levels
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers; Framework Agreements for recurring items; competitive selection of Independent Verification Agencies (IVAs); Open Contracting Data Standard for transparency; debarment/suspension list compliance
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Multi-source M&E framework anchored by NPC at FME; national learning assessments at baseline and Year 4; Annual School Census (ASC) digitalization; local-level learning evaluations for Grades 1-2; third-party independent verification of DLIs; annual basic education status reports; grievance redress mechanism with SERVICOM support

identified_risks.risk_description: High level of state autonomy with concurrent and overlapping responsibilities for basic education across federal, state, and local government levels leads to coordination challenges. Recent decision to grant political and financial autonomy to local governments may exacerbate coordination issues.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engage all stakeholders including Nigerian Governors Forum (NGF) and Association of Local Governments of Nigeria (ALGON); adopt sector-wide approach (SWAp); leverage new FME leadership for close engagement with relevant agencies; establish clear institutional arrangements and roles

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertain growth outlook heavily reliant on external factors and government policy response. Limited resources available for education sector; potential to exacerbate inflation and trigger inflation-depreciation spiral.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Monitor macroeconomic outlook and support government through interventions; strengthen macroeconomic policy framework through separate engagements (RESET Development Policy Financing and ARMOR PforR); use DLIs to incentivize increased funding from federal and state governments

identified_risks.risk_description: Coordination, accountability and capacity issues between FME, UBEC, SME and SUBEB may result in delayed program implementation. Sequencing and coordination of interdependent HOPE Programs may cause delays with repercussions across operations. Independent nature of participating states poses additional coordination challenges.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Establish Inter-Ministerial HOPE Steering Committee for improved coordination; provide targeted TA under IPF component to enhance implementation, coordination and M&E capacity; coordinate closely between HOPE-EDU and HOPE-GOV on productive sequencing; engage frequently with NGF; establish compact agreement between federal and states

identified_risks.risk_description: Delayed budget releases from states to implementing agencies; low budget execution rates; weak internal audit function lacking technical capacity; use of manual accounting systems at UBEC and SUBEBs; delayed financial report submissions; inability of states to provide counterpart funds; late submission of audited financial statements; ambiguity on operational procedures; low procurement capacity; lack of competition in procuring recurring items; inadequate KPI measurement; non-publication of contract awards; non-application of World Bank debarment lists; poor inter-agency coordination.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: HOPE-GOV DLI 2 incentivizes increased budget execution rates; establish service standards for timely fund release (within 14 days); centralize internal audit function at FPFMD/OAGF; deploy accounting software at UBEC within 3 months; use HOPE-GOV eligibility criteria for timely audit submissions; implement Procurement Capacity Development Plan; use Framework Agreements for recurring items; monitor fiduciary KPIs; publish procurement data per Open Contracting Data Standard; enforce World Bank debarment/suspension list compliance; establish clear institutional arrangements; submit bi-annual corruption reports

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential environmental risks from construction activities including noise, dust, vegetation clearance, soil erosion, accidents. Social risks include land acquisition, exclusion of vulnerable groups, limited grievance mechanism access, child labor, gender-based violence (GBV), sexual exploitation and abuse/sexual harassment (SEA/SH), and security risks. E-waste from digital devices.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implement ESSA recommendations; hire environmental, social and GBV specialists within 3 months; develop and implement Labor Management Procedures; develop E&S Screening Checklist; establish grievance mechanism within 3 months with SERVICOM support; develop school-level GBV protocols and implement program-specific GRM within 6 months; develop e-waste management strategies within 1 year; develop community land donation protocol within 3 months; incorporate climate-resilient standards in construction; include secure perimeters and gender-segregated WASH facilities; establish SEA/SH-GBV protocols in schools

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Program-specific grievance mechanism to be established within 3 months of effectiveness; existing program grievance mechanism available; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available; World Bank Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) available; call center for feedback on program activities; percentage of grievances addressed within specified timeline tracked as indicator
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender audit of TLMs to remove stereotypes; teacher training on gender-aware active learning; disaggregated learning evaluation results by gender; community mobilization for out-of-school girls enrollment with higher financial incentives for girls in NFBE; secure perimeters and WASH facilities for girls in new classrooms; adoption of SEA/SH-GBV protocols in schools and NFLCs; gender-based planning and budgeting in UBE IF guidelines; female mentor promotion; gender-disaggregated ASC data collection
vulnerable_groups: Out-of-school children (14.8 million aged 5-14 years, 95% in Northern zones)
Girls (lower enrollment rates in Northern states; at risk of SEA/SH and GBV; targeted by Boko Haram)
Children with disabilities (44% of girls with disabilities out of school)
Children in non-formal education (only 44% are girls)
Poorest households (net primary attendance 30+ percentage points lower than richest quintile)
Rural children (net primary attendance 25 percentage points lower than urban)
Children in climate-vulnerable areas (floods, droughts, extreme heat)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Program addresses conflict-related barriers to education: security concerns cited by 1.6-3.1% of families as reason for non-enrollment; Boko Haram targeting of female students in northern Nigeria creating reluctance to enroll girls; program includes measures to ensure safe passage to/from schools (e.g., 'walking school bus'); establishes secure school perimeters; develops SEA/SH-GBV protocols and linkages to state-level reporting and case management systems; incorporates child protection and security awareness in NFLC management committee training; includes climate awareness and disaster response planning to address climate-induced displacement
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria designated as Fragile State(s) in operation datasheet. Key fragility factors: (1) High learning poverty - 76% learning deprivation rate, 45.8 million children unable to read simple text; (2) Large out-of-school population - 14.8 million OOSC (world's largest), 95% in Northern zones; (3) Weak governance - poor coordination between federal/state/local levels, cumbersome UBEIF access procedures, lack of transparency and accountability; (4) Low public investment - 1.2% of GDP on education (among world's lowest), 10% of national budget; (5) Institutional capacity gaps - weak internal audit, manual accounting systems, delayed financial reporting; (6) Climate vulnerability - ranked 152/187 on ND-GAIN index, low adaptive capacity; (7) Security challenges - Boko Haram activities, safety concerns affecting school enrollment; (8) Macroeconomic uncertainty - heavily oil-dependent, limited resources for education
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks identified: Boko Haram targeting of female students in northern Nigeria; 1.6% of rural and 3.1% of urban families cite safety/security concerns as reasons for not sending children to school; program includes measures to address security: secure school perimeters for new classrooms; safe passage mechanisms ('walking school bus'); security awareness and response training for NFLC management committees; SEA/SH-GBV protocols with linkages to state-level reporting systems; community management committees responsible for security awareness and response
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Program addresses displacement indirectly through climate resilience measures: school infrastructure designed to serve as refuge for persons displaced by extreme climate events; emergency evacuation plans required in partnership agreements for new classrooms; disaster risk reduction and early warning systems incorporated in school designs; climate-informed site selection to avoid flood-prone areas; program supports access to education for vulnerable populations including those affected by climate shocks and natural disasters; 2022 floods displaced 2.8 million people across Nigeria, highlighting need for climate-resilient education infrastructure
